speaker: interviewer
text: --see for now about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, do have something-- you wanted to read something?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, there's two ways we can do this. We can either-- the poetry, we can-- is it written down on a piece of paper, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It is written down. But, but it has to do with letting go because I was getting really hyper and tense. Because I've done a lot of this. I speak for the Holocaust. I have something else. And I'm writing this story, the three--

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're at tape 4. And we're interviewing Mr. George Scott. I'd like to ask you, why has it taken now until you started talking about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, interestingly through the accumulation of my own years and each season bringing each-- bring-- each season bringing new things to the surface, you could almost say naturally, the types of reflections and the types of sensitivities I, I now possess, were overridden by more immediate concerns, that is, earning a living. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, a number of reasons. First is that it offends me to think that I'm ashamed that I'm a Jew. I haven't yet seen a person in this world who wasn't a something. And I am neither more, nor less. I, too, am a something. And I, I am affiliated to a group of people known as Jews. My mother was a Jew-- Jewess. My father was Jewish and, and all through the generations. And knowing a little bit about our history, I don't think comparing our history to anyone's history that I am justified to, to feel ashamed, even though I have deeply absorbed the wounds of pre-Hitler propaganda where I looked for-- I caught myself looking for so-called Jewish traits in my fellow Jews. And, and I find that hurtful. And this is an aside. My entire life is an aside. I happen to be reading, just now, Sartre's And he shows an amazing insight into human nature, into my-- even into my own nature. And, and I feel enlightened to, to admit to some of the observations that he makes. It also happens that my grandmother's youngest sister's son, who's in Toronto, who has a Jewish wife and two children, they, they became Christians in 1943. And I see them. I love them. But they want to have nothing to do with being Jewish. And their kids married Christian kids. And that's fine. But in a sense, it-- I feel that-- because of them, I feel I want to stand up and say that I'm a Jew. Why I haven't talked to my children about this-- when I started working for Ontario Hydro by the name of Scott because I didn't-- certainly, I wasn't [NON-ENGLISH]. And I didn't want to call myself Szasz or Spiegel. I didn't want them to know I was Jewish. I was scared. It's understandable. I said I was Protestant. And-- and that was fine. But as time went on, I did admit to people who I worked with in Hydro. And Hydro was a really boring job, in terms of being mechanical. But the people were wonderful. I had a couple of Englishmen, a Canadian. And they were like brothers to me-- good sense of humor. Some of them were in the army in the war. And we got along great. And I told them that yes, I was Jewish. And with-- in terms of having children-- and we lived in Scarborough because I could not afford to live in a Jewish area in Toronto. I could have bought a, a, a semi-detached place on Keele Street. And, for the same amount of money-- $3,500, which I borrowed from the Ontario Hydro Credit Union-- I managed to get a four-bedroom bungalow at Crocus Drive. That's Warden and 401. And my three children-- or two of my three children were born there. And, and people knew that we were Jewish. But I didn't talk about the Holocaust to my children. Primarily, in the first instance, they were too-- too young. In the second instance, when they got older, I didn't want to weaken them. I didn't think that this, in any way, could contribute to them. My huge-- and to this very moment, inexplicable-- embarrassment with the human race, how these things could, in fact, go on-- and it wasn't one Hitler or, or 10 people, diabolical, got together. There were tens of thousands of people involved in the-- in the production of, of, of the death factories one way or another, and millions of people who didn't care and didn't want to care. And even though all along I'm saying that these people are my brothers-- and I do not ever want to forgo them. And if they're part of me, I must take responsibility for them. It's a difficult thing to do. For all these reasons, I didn't talk to my kids about it. I didn't want them to feel any less than the rest of the kids who they were playing with.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they react when you did tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It came by degrees. By then, they were married. And they were surprised. But-- and you will meet them later. They are wonderful children. They have-- two of them have children of their own. And the youngest one is still not married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're good friends. We love each other. We respect each other. We were-- we were always honest with each other. And I think that's so important for, for a human. And be there as close as a child of your very own, there's still a different universe unto themselves. And unless the attempt is made to connect with them, to, to relish them, to make them feel secure, to encourage them, to, to give signs of their own being, whether through play or speaking, or anything-- it is so, so important. And because I had nothing when I came here, and because my wife, who had her own baggage out of Glasgow, Scotland-- she was 18 when she got here out of a very unhappy relationship on her mother and father's side. You know-- you know, the kind of baggage that people carry has a way of sapping our energies. And we certainly didn't want to transmit it to our children. I mean, there are personal baggages. Holocaust is not a personal baggage, even though it is. It's really a collective load that somehow or other must be explained away or understood or guarded against. And, and, and, and the bizarre folly of the entire thing must be seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, when these cities had signs on that such-and-such a place now is Judenfrei-- hey, there are no more Jews here. Did they-- that mean that the people were happy? Or they had no problems? I mean, it's-- the least amount of thought should have told these people that that's, that's stupid. That's, that's, that's ridiculous.

speaker: interviewee
text: The achievement of inner balance, of feeling good about oneself, can only come from within. And it can only come through the kinds of diets which are fed to us or which we are lucky enough to acquire by our own efforts. We can choose the kind of books we want to read. We can choose the kinds of friends we want-- who are able to receive us and who are open enough to allow them to be received. And that is an open-ended thing here. And that's why I'm so happy to be here. And when a friend of mine, who was actually a business competitor-- not competitor. I'm not in his league. He's very successful. [LAUGHS] His name is Max Eisen, who owns Looseleaf, a very large paper company, said that he's speaking for the Holocaust. And to my amazement, I heard him speak. And he spoke of the boys after the war. And I always speak of the boys from the orphanage. And I listened to him. And it was so quaint to see this old man talk about the boys. But it resonated something in me. And he said, come on, George, you go. And you speak. And I went to the Toronto Holocaust center. And since then, I'm meeting school kids. And it's, it's really a, a powerful experience. Now, I don't stand there droning about who stood on my toes. These kids, I watch them. They fall asleep. I talk about themselves. I talk about how happy I am to see them, how, how great it is to be who they are, what a tremendous chance they got. And I do tell them the dreadful things that happened to me. There's hardly ever any question.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when I tell them, when was it the last time that you looked in the mirror and looked into your own eyes? You know, there's a lot of power there. And I tell them, don't look in your eyes as if you're fixing your tie or doing you hair, or putting on lipstick. But look in your eyes with the intent to see yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, people-- the kids are listening. Because I think my only significance can only be to the extent that I can involve myself into their lives. And I never, ever talk about [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] shit, bad things. I always talk about opportunities. Because when I look upon my own life, the kinds of things that made me strong-- and it's not even optional-- were the kinds of things that made me feel good about myself, that made me think that maybe one day I can have a car. Maybe one day I am able-- even though I have nothing, one day I can buy a house. One day I can have a family. And-- and-- and in a sense, give them all the things that I wish I had received myself, as best as I knew how.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does the music provide for you, your music?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Music had always been-- you know, it-- it's like-- it seems to me that when, when a person is born, they're looking for things to grab onto. And I was-- well, there was a chestnut tree in my grandparents' place. And there were the roosters. And, and there was the rain falling. And there was music. I watched the peasant women dance and sing. And I, I learned the songs. And I sang them. And they were pleased to hear me sing. And they thought I was funny. And it, it meant that I got discovered. I was-- I was there. I was in some way part of that group. And then, later in the orphanage, I played a mouth organ. I played an accordion. And I tried to play piano. And that's why I admired [NON-ENGLISH], who, as I said earlier, was shot into the Danube, one of the boys. He was a couple of years older than I, a bit eccentric, but, you know, he could make that piano talk. And I just couldn't understand how fingers could achieve that. Music, to me, was something that I always responded to. There is the beat. There is the, the melody. There is the, the universal cry of elation or reflection or, or-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] epic parts where music speaks. And, and whatever music does, it's, it's always been readily part of me. I understood it. And I knew that that sound, everyone and anyone could hear and, and, and relate to it. And, and, and there are such amazing landscape artists in the figure of Vivaldi or Bach or Brahms or Schubert or Mozart or Mendelssohn or modern people, as well. Excuse me. A familiarity, in a sense, helps me-- has always helped me to identify what is it that I was doing. And that applies for music. The kind of music that is less familiar, is-- I can relate to it less. I-- but there is such a vast field in music. And, and since I have learned from within myself-- and most things I had learned from within myself that I know, that I can speak of with some conviction-- that, that it is the emotional base within me that responds, and the intellectual segment which translates and rationalizes and makes me feel part of whatever I'm listening to. And I know that these faculties exist in, in other humans other than myself. It, it presents to me a, a, a, a, a marvelous opportunity for everyone who wishes to speak, to speak; and for everyone who wants to, to listen, to listen; and for everyone to hear, that they can be heard, that, that, that they're here. They're real. There is a lot in the stresses that I know from living in Canada that, that tends to cancel a person's identity. It almost appears to be a, a-- an offering of refuge, which is, really, non-identity. It's not a refuge. And the years pile up on years. And choices must be made. And I, I, I think that it is-- it would be great if we were better prepared by our schools, by our parents, and if their parents had been better prepared to make better choices for, for life itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also do some writing. Can you read what you've written, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank you. I, I write practically nonstop. And I usually write, in a sense, to some-- either to explain some bottlenecks within myself or to elaborate in some notions which I have of why things are the way they are. I cannot remember a time, bar none, that I was not interested in process. And one thing had always led to another thing. And to me, this gives me a lot of satisfaction to, to think about these things. This is something that I had written. I haven't given it a title because I was getting headaches. I knew about this interview today. And I, I didn't like to be so off-balance the way I was. And I had-- my head was pounding. I couldn't sleep. So I wrote this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 'It's still February the 15th, 1995. What a day. What a headache-- and then the cure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sit by a river and watch. Why is it beautiful? Listen to the unending music of the waves. Why is it ever new? How graciously the water moves. How it lets go. How it doesn't want to stop or hold onto anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How it acknowledges the terrain and moves on. How it never stops singing. How it cares not to remember its tune. How it knows its channel and is contained by it. How it remains free from reflecting on itself. How it is never drowned by its own waters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let it all go. Watch. Forgive. Listen. Let it flow.'

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you. I thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just give me the cue to go. And then I'll start. The picture we are looking at on the left is my father. On the right is my mother. She is semi-pregnant with me. So it must be 1929. They were very much in love. And they both had a kind of-- from what I hear from other people-- a kind of a sensitive, romantic way of looking at the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what was your father's name? And what was your mother's name?

words.text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was called Emil Spiegel. And my mother's maiden name was Sarah Reich, the fourth girl in the Reich family. The oval picture-- the lady in the center is my mother. The-- to the-- to the right of her is a friend. To the left of her is her youngest sister, Klara. The next small square picture is my grandmother. The center picture, the square picture, is my beautiful grandmother, Ilona Wohl. She's the sister of the man who saved my life in Burkinau. The picture is taken in Szucsi in around 1937 on the bench where most of these pictures had been taken. The lady on the left is my aunt, Yollie. She is the third daughter of the Reich family. Next to her is, is cousin [NON-ENGLISH], who is the same age as I am-- or was the same age as I. And next to her is her father. Their name is [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. And they were in [NON-ENGLISH]. They were in the wood business. This amazing lady is my Aunt Bertha, the only of the six daughters who survived. She was like a mother to me. She always did what one was supposed to do and never, ever talked about it. She was 91 when she died a few years ago in [NON-ENGLISH]. This picture is-- on the left is my grandmother. Next to her is cousin [NON-ENGLISH]. And behind her is Aunt Margo. Next to Aunt Margo is my grandfather, with his immense mustache. And right in front of him is reluctant me. I remember being jealous that I didn't have a mother and [NON-ENGLISH] did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your grandmother and grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother's name was Ilona. And my grandfather's first name was Mondi, M-O-N-D-I, Reich. But my grandmother was a Wohl girl to begin with. This picture is the picture of the orphanage, [NON-ENGLISH] 25 Budapest. The year is approximately 1946. I am not on this picture, but many of the teachers, et cetera, who I know are on it. On the left is the teacher, [? Lieberman-- ?] on the very left. On the very right is Mr. [? Wolf, ?] who I had here on a visit many years ago. In the middle is the [NON-ENGLISH] called Mr. [? Schwartz ?] with the two kids. And Mrs. Pfeifer, the person who looked after the, the sewing room-- as I said earlier, who was really a fierce lady-- is in the second row left next to Miss [NON-ENGLISH] who was the one in charge of the younger kids. You can see the uniforms and, and the building in the background. Those were happy times. This is a, a picture perhaps the 1st of May, 1945. I am the third person from the left with the hat waving. As I said before, we got into Dachau in the evening. And this is the morning of the following day. What you cannot see on the picture is on-- to the left of me there are three or four corpses lying on the ground. And behind us, which you cannot see from the people, there was a huge mountain of-- a mountain of corpses lying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I, I, I never really, as I said before, spent too much time thinking before about the concentration years. But my wife was saying, as we were about to discard some newspapers, were you not in Dachau? And I said, yes. And then, as you can see--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the reverse side of this picture, there is the article in the that caught my attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just describe to us how you--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --came across this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, am I still on?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This photograph was in a I don't know, about six or seven years ago. My wife asks me, were you not in Dachau? And I said, yes. And then, as, as I looked at the picture, the headline said, 'Isn't it time yet to bring Nazi war criminals to justice?' And underneath, lo and behold, I couldn't believe my eyes. I was frozen. There I stood, waving. And I wrote to writers. And I told them. And they sent me this very nice, glossy photograph. And they also congratulated me. And my children, who were looking for big bucks, every one of them got a copy of this picture. I really don't know what they're going to do with it. The picture we're looking at-- on the left is my beaming, rather prominent forehead. On my lap is my daughter, Shirley Ilona. Next to her is my-- our youngest boy, Alan David, held by his late mother, my beautiful wife. Ruth Blanche Levstein was her married-- single name. And right next to her stands my oldest son, Martin Emil. And the vintage is circa 1964. This picture is of my beautiful, incredible wife, [? 'Hatsy' ?] Harriet Brav-Baum. She is the oldest daughter of Rabbi Brav-Baum, a reformed rabbi. And I'm lucky to have her.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you marry her?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married approximately five years ago, I guess, 1989 or thereabouts. But we have known each other for some years prior to that. The picture we are seeing is my oldest son, Martin Emil, and his beautiful wife, [? Rita. ?] [? Rita ?] is from Russia. And she's a beautiful girl. Excuse me. Here I stand with my oldest son, again, Martin, and one of my beautiful granddaughters, [? Leanna. ?] This is the incredible-- who is now four-year-old-- [? Jake, ?] Martin's other son. He is-- he's an ongoing delight and puzzlement. And he's a growing concern. This is-- this is my wonderful grandson, my third grandchild, [NON-ENGLISH]. OK, these amazingly delightful damsels are the daughters of my beautiful daughter, Shirley-- [? Rachel ?] and [? Sarah. ?] Words cannot convey how lucky and happy and honored I feel to have had such unbelievable children like the ones sitting around me. On my right is Martin. He is the oldest. Behind me is Shirley. And next to her is Alan David. Alan David was 35 a month or so ago. And Martin is at the ripe age of 40. And Shirley's in the middle. Shirley's a schoolteacher. Martin works with me. And Alan is an artist. We have kind of-- try to keep in touch growing up together. And I am sure it's been a, a, a great challenge to them. And the beat goes on. But we've always loved each other. And there had always been a very deep respect amongst us for one another. And I am sure that I'm not only speaking for myself when I say that every one of us appreciates who we are, and where we are, and what we are. It's-- it's an unbelievable, inconceivable event that I am now sitting here in 1995, after what I told you about 1944. And, and it proves to me that trees do bear fruit and, and the good that we are for, it is possible to pursue-- not only possible, but I feel bound, as I am sure there are millions in this world, if not billions, bound to pursue that good, that common good that takes the limits off the, the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the stops of-- of-- of going forward, of discovering, of growing, of being, of living our own days out as best as possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I just want to say that I'm really grateful. I've been the baby in the family. And I've watched the family grow and watched my father grow. And I've just developed a real reverence for life. And I'm really happy. And I'm happy my father survived. And I hope that this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] show that we're putting on, this presentation, will offer strength and courage to those who watch it and see that anything is possible. And I'm just really happy he survived and that we're all here. And we have great lives now. And I hope to pass it on to my children and them to theirs.
